title: Orbit CLI — Documentation
subtitle: Orbit is a zero-config deploy tool. This is the complete reference: install it, wire a project, and ship. Every page is generated from one JSON content model and renders fully offline.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.summary: What Orbit is and the problem it solves.

blocks.text: Orbit turns a folder into a running service with one command. No YAML sprawl, no bespoke CI — it infers the build, provisions the runtime, and streams logs back to your terminal.
It works by detecting your project type, building an immutable image, and rolling it out behind a managed edge. You interact with it entirely through the `orbit` CLI.

blocks.title: Mental model
blocks.body: Think of Orbit as `git push` for infrastructure: you describe *what* to run, and it figures out *how*.

sections.title: Installation
sections.summary: Install the CLI on macOS, Linux, or Windows.

blocks.text: Install the CLI with the one-line script (macOS / Linux) or via the package managers below.

blocks.title: install.sh
blocks.code:
curl -fsSL https://get.orbit.dev | sh

# or with Homebrew
brew install orbit-cli

# verify
orbit --version

blocks.title: Windows
blocks.body: On Windows, install through `winget install Orbit.CLI` — the shell script is POSIX-only.

sections.title: Quickstart
sections.summary: From an empty folder to a live URL in four steps.

blocks.text: The fastest path from nothing to a deployed service:

steps.title: Log in
steps.body: Run `orbit login` and complete the browser flow. Your token is stored in the OS keychain.

steps.title: Link the project
steps.body: In your project folder run `orbit link` — Orbit detects the framework and writes a minimal `orbit.json`.

steps.title: Deploy
steps.body: `orbit deploy` builds an image and rolls it out. The live URL is printed when the rollout is healthy.

steps.title: Watch logs
steps.body: `orbit logs --follow` streams structured logs from every running instance.

blocks.text: The first deploy is slower because the base image is cached afterwards. Subsequent deploys are typically under 20 seconds.

sections.title: Configuration
sections.summary: The orbit.json fields and their defaults.

blocks.text: `orbit link` writes a minimal config; every field is optional and has a sane default.

rows: The build command. Inferred from the framework if omitted.
The command that starts the server. Must bind to `$PORT`.
Edge regions to run in. Defaults to the nearest region to you.
A map of environment variables baked into the image at build time.

sections.title: The deploy pipeline
sections.summary: What happens between `orbit deploy` and a live URL.

blocks.text: Every deploy runs the same five-stage pipeline. If any stage fails, the previous release keeps serving traffic — deploys are atomic.

blocks.caption: The deploy pipeline: a failed stage aborts the rollout and the previous release keeps serving.
blocks.code:
flowchart LR
  A([orbit deploy]) --> B[detect + build]
  B --> C[[push image]]
  C --> D{health check}
  D -->|healthy| E[shift traffic]
  D -->|unhealthy| F([rollback])
  E --> G([live URL])

rows: Uploads the image layers not already cached
Atomically shifts the edge to the new release
Build and roll out the current project.
Instantly revert to the previous release.

sections.title: Exit codes
sections.summary: How to interpret a non-zero exit.

rows: Generic error — see the message above the code.
Build failed — inspect the build logs.

blocks.title: Scripting
blocks.body: In CI, check the exit code rather than parsing stdout — the human-readable output is not a stable contract.
